figures.caption: Figure 6: t-SNE visualization of tag embeddings. (a) Existing datasets exhibit a dense, entangled distribution where tags are indistinguishable. (b) In contrast, StickerBench shows a highly structured distribution with clear separation between clusters. Here, each distinct color corresponds to one of the six attributes in our schema (Appearance, Emotion, Action, Camera Composition, Style, Background), visually validating that our dataset effectively disentangles semantic components compared to the baseline.

Figure 8: Qualitative results of SEAL integrated into representative TTF personalization methods (CoRe (Wu et al., 2025) and UnZipLoRA (Liu et al., 2025)) in the one-shot setting. For each concept, the leftmost image is the single reference input, and the remaining images are generated samples conditioned on the prompt shown below each row. Across diverse attribute edits, the module-integrated variants preserve concept identity while maintaining contextual controllability, indicating reduced visual entanglement and alleviated structural rigidity.

Figure 7: Qualitative comparison of baseline personalization methods and their SEALintegrated variants in single-image sticker personalization. While baselines often exhibit visual entanglement or structural rigidity, integrating SEAL improves identity disentanglement and contextual controllability under the same prompts.

Table 1: LLaVA-based prompting protocol for structured tag annotation in StickerBench. The first field <domain> (animation or real) is used only for domain filtering and is excluded from the six-attribute schema. We map <character> to Appearance and <composition> to Camera Composition.

Figure 11: Inference-time visualization of cross-attention maps across different K values. The visualization uses the embeddings learned with the Split-merge Token Strategy. When K = 1, the model fails to capture diverse attributes of the target concept, resulting in degraded identity preservation. In contrast, our method (K = 5) yields more coherent attention behavior across layers (e.g., Layer 0 and Layer 4), enabling the concept to be synthesized in diverse contexts while preserving its core identity.

Figure 10: Visual analysis of structural rigidity with respect to Structure-aware Layer Restriction during embedding adaptation. Updating shallow cross-attention layers (e.g., Layer 0) that primarily capture low-level structural patterns causes the model to overfit to reference edges, resulting in a fixed layout. In contrast, applying the spatial constraint to deeper, semantically oriented cross-attention layers (e.g., Layer 4) reduces layout memorization and restores contextual controllability under attribute-level prompt edits.
Table 2: Comparison of StickerBench with prior datasets. Our dataset provides superior scale, resolution, and attribute richness.

Table 3: Integration results on StickerBench (one-shot). We report representative baselines and the effect of integrating SEAL into selected TTF methods. Improved values over the corresponding baseline are highlighted in blue with the absolute gain in parentheses.

Figure 1: Comparison of cross-attention maps. We visualize spatial cross-attention maps of the concept token from an image-near cross-attention layer (Layer 0), which tends to emphasize low-level spatial patterns, and a semantically informative cross-attention layer (Layer 4). Baselines (top) such as DreamBooth (Ruiz et al., 2023) and CoRe (Wu et al., 2025) exhibit rigid patterns in Layer 0, leading to (i) visual entanglement (red circle) and (ii) structural rigidity (red rectangle). In contrast, our semantic-aware S∗ (bottom) suppresses these artifacts by enforcing object-aligned attention and reducing the influence of structurally biased image-near layers, thereby mitigating structural rigidity, improving identity disentanglement from the background, and restoring contextual controllability under attribute-level prompt edits.

Figure 12: Detailed analysis of cross-attention maps at the end of embedding adaptation (250 steps) and at inference. We compare optimization stability between K = 5 (top, blue box) and K = 1 (bottom, red box) using attention maps from Layer 4. For K = 5, attention patterns for context tokens remain consistent when the category token (e.g., “Character”) is replaced by the concept token S∗, indicating stable semantic injection. This stability helps capture richer attributes and improves identity preservation at inference compared to K = 1.

Figure 3: Overview of our proposed dataset construction pipeline. The framework consists of several key stages, from Image Parsing and Filtering to Tag Generation and Superresolution, ultimately producing the high-quality StickerBench dataset.

Table 4: Ablation study of the proposed adaptation module on StickerBench for singleimage sticker personalization, using CoRe (Wu et al., 2025) as the underlying personalization baseline. We evaluate the contribution of Structure-aware Layer Restriction, Semantic-guided Spatial Attention Loss, and the Split-merge Token Strategy. Best results are bolded, and second-best results are underlined.

Figure 5: Comparison of intra-caption tag similarity histograms based on CLIP text embeddings. (a) Existing datasets like Sticker-queries exhibit high cosine similarity (Mean=0.77), indicating significant semantic redundancy within captions. (b) In contrast, StickerBench shows a lower mean similarity (Mean=0.71). This demonstrates that our six-attribute schema effectively enforces semantic diversity, constructing captions with high information density and minimal redundancy.
Figure 2: Overview of SEAL as a plug-and-play semantic adaptation module for singleimage sticker personalization.

Figure 9: Visual ablation study of SEAL on StickerBench for single-image sticker personalization using CoRe (Wu et al., 2025) as the underlying personalization baseline. We show generated samples (top) and cross-attention maps of the concept token (bottom). Removing Structure-aware Layer Restriction increases structural rigidity, while removing the Semantic-guided Spatial Attention Loss increases visual entanglement. Removing the Split-merge Token Strategy reduces optimization stability under one-shot supervision, leading to less attribute-consistent attention and degraded identity preservation. The full module improves identity disentanglement and contextual controllability.

Figure 4: Examples from Sticker-Queries (Chee et al., 2025b). The associated text annotations are short, informal, and highly variable in form, often consisting of exclamations, repeated words, or emoticons rather than structured semantic descriptions. Such annotations provide limited support for attribute-level prompt construction and controlled evaluation in single-image sticker personalization.
